briefing.opening: Good morning. We will now begin the climate briefing for June 14, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis and Increasing Vulnerability
sections.content: News reported today warns that the climate crisis is deepening globally, leading to an increase in various social and environmental vulnerabilities. Mongolia is on the front lines of the climate crisis, experiencing both transformation into dry, frozen land and unusual heavy rainfall [1, 21]. Domestically, climate change is rapidly emerging as a new risk alongside the spread of AI and changes in population structure [2]. Fisheries and food security also face the climate crisis, making it urgent to find countermeasures [5, 8]. The Ministry of Health and Welfare is expediting preparations for extreme heat, including establishing a 'Severe Heatwave Alert' and strengthening protection for vulnerable groups [6, 35]. In particular, global coral reefs are threatened by global warming [28], and warnings that the collapse of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) could change Europe's climate 10 times faster than expected underscore the global severity of the situation [34].

sections.title: 🌱 Expanding Domestic Efforts for Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Amidst this crisis, positive movements for carbon neutrality are actively spreading across various regions in Korea. Yongin City is leading a library-based carbon neutrality practice culture by creating eco-friendly libraries through 'Green Library' operations and promoting tumbler use and shared bookshelves [4, 9, 14, 15]. Youth in the Mungyeong area participated in EM mud ball throwing activities [10], while citizens in Gumi and Gwangmyeong cities actively engaged in carbon neutrality practices through Environment Day and Environmental Education Week events [13, 16]. Furthermore, Seoul City has launched an innovative program linking 'Ttareungi' (public bicycle) commutes with carbon neutrality donations [12], and the Chungnam Provincial Council is striving to strengthen policy foundations, laying the groundwork for achieving carbon neutrality and green growth by 2045 [11].

sections.title: 🔋 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Economic Opportunities
sections.content: Positive news continues in the energy transition sector, which is central to addressing the climate crisis. Arguments have been made for guiding regional energy systems towards renewable energy and SMRs (Small Modular Reactors), and there is a growing call to integrate renewable energy power plants into grid operations [17, 18]. These transition efforts are also leading to economic opportunities; the UK has demonstrated that its net-zero economy is worth £105 billion, supporting 1.1 million jobs [19]. Hanwha Solutions, a domestic company, also recorded strong performance in the second quarter, driven by co-growth in its chemical and solar divisions, proving the growth potential of renewable energy-related industries [20].

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Response and Policy Trends
sections.content: The international community and various regions are taking diverse approaches to climate change adaptation and policy responses. Mongolia, on the front lines of the climate crisis, is exploring long-term solutions by choosing to plant 'trees' [1], and Vietnam is expanding cooperation in sustainable agriculture and climate change adaptation research [7]. Meanwhile, Jeonbuk Province is making efforts to protect fisheries, including releasing 200,000 juvenile blue crabs in response to declining catches along the West Sea coast [22]. In the United States, a judge appointed by Biden ordered the restoration of climate change-related mentions in national parks [23, 32], indicating attempts at policy shifts, while Louisiana's move to outlaw lawsuits against oil companies over climate change suggests active legal and policy debates [29]. Environment and Climate Change Canada is assisting climate change adaptation by providing proactive information through the release of its summer seasonal outlook [33].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the severity of the global climate crisis, along with various domestic and international efforts and opportunities to overcome it. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: [메마르고 얼어붙은 땅 몽골①]기후위기 최전선, 몽골의 선택은 ‘나무’
articles.description: On May 27, children were planting trees in Bayanchogtsom, Tuv Province, Mongolia. (Bayanchogtsom | Reporter Ban Ki-woong) On the afternoon of the 26th of last month, in Ulaanbaatar, the capital of Mongolia, a sudden heavy downpour began after leaving the airport and entering the city center. In Mongolia, where annual rainfall is barely 100-350mm, rain is typically a sign of life. However, due to climate change, heavy rainfall has become frequent, leading to flood damage...

summary.phenomenon: On the afternoon of the 26th of last month, an unusual heavy downpour occurred in Ulaanbaatar, the capital of Mongolia, with intense rain far exceeding the typical annual precipitation of 100-350mm.
summary.cause: Due to climate change, heavy rainfall has become frequent in Mongolia, leading to an increase in flood damage.
summary.outlook: Mongolia has chosen tree planting as a response to the climate crisis. On May 27, children participated in tree planting activities in Bayanchogtsom, Tuv Province, as part of this effort.

articles.original_description: 지난 5월27일 몽골 투브아이막 바양척트솜에서 어린이들이 나무를 심고 있다. 바양척트 | 반기웅 기자지난달 26일 오후 몽골 수도 울란바타르. 공항을 떠나 도심에 접어들자 느닷없이 거센 비가 쏟아졌다. 한 해 내리는 비의 양이 100~350㎜ 남짓인 몽골에서 비는 생명을 살리는 신호였다. 그러나 기후변화로 집중 호우가 빈번해지고 홍수 피해가 ···

articles.title: Jeollabuk-do Province Releases 200,000 Juvenile Blue Crabs
articles.original_title: 전북도, 어린 꽃게 20만마리 방류
articles.description: Blue crabs, a key income species for West Coast fisheries, are facing declining catches, prompting intensive efforts for resource recovery. Amidst the reduction of marine resources along the West Coast due to various climate changes, including unusually cold winters leading to low sea temperatures in spring, Jeollabuk-do Province has initiated resource recovery by releasing 200,000 juvenile blue crabs. The Jeollabuk-do Fisheries Technology Research Institute released 200,000 self-produced juvenile blue crabs, each with a carapace width of 1cm or more, with 100,000 released in Gusi-po, Gochang, and others in Bangchukdo, Gunsan, and Buan.

summary.phenomenon: The catch of blue crabs, a representative income species for fisheries along the West Coast, is decreasing. In response, Jeollabuk-do Province released 200,000 juvenile blue crabs, each with a carapace width of 1cm or more, in areas such as Gusi-po in Gochang, Bangchukdo in Gunsan, and Buan.
summary.cause: Various climate changes, including unusually cold winters leading to low sea temperatures in spring, are identified as the cause of the decline in marine resources along the West Coast.
summary.outlook: Jeollabuk-do Province is striving, through the release of juvenile blue crabs, to restore marine resources along the West Coast and address the issue of declining catches.

articles.original_description: 서해안 어업 대표 소득 어종…어획량 감소에 자원 회복 안간힘겨울철 이상한파에 따른 봄철 바다 저수온 현상을 비롯한 다양한 기후변화로 서해 연안 수산자원이 감소하고 있는 가운데 전북도가 어린 꽃게 20만마리를 방류하며 자원 회복에 나섰다.전북도 수산기술연구소는 자체 생산한 갑폭 1㎝ 이상의 어린 꽃게 20만마리를 고창 구시포에 10만마리, 군산 방축도와 부안···

articles.original_title: Amoc collapse could change Europe’s climate 10x faster than expected. We aren’t ready
articles.description: The system of ocean current that moves heat in the Atlantic Ocean plays a key role in regulating climate. Today’s monitoring of it may be discontinuedImagine we detect a large asteroid heading straight for Earth. We are able to intervene and prevent disaster, but instead we cut the funding needed to track it. A few million dollars, it was argued, was too expensive to have a chance to save society.While this scenario isn’t real, the metaphor is alarmingly accurate. In Europe, we spend €1bn to mon

summary.phenomenon: Monitoring of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC), an ocean current system that transports heat in the Atlantic Ocean, is at risk of being discontinued.
summary.cause: While AMOC plays a crucial role in regulating climate, the funding required to track it is at risk of being cut.
summary.outlook: Concerns are being raised that the collapse of AMOC could change Europe's climate 10 times faster than anticipated, and preparations for this are insufficient.

articles.original_title: ‘It’s going to be extremely hot’: workers imperiled as sweltering World Cup temperatures are forecast
articles.description: It could top 90F in several cities hosting World Cup games – and workers could pay the price with their healthAs the World Cup kicks off, labor advocates and scholars warn that the workers making the tournament possible could face serious heat-related risks.“It’s going to be extremely hot, and you just cannot leave people unprotected or you’re going to deal with a lot of injuries,” said Jonathan Alingu, co-executive director of Central Florida Jobs With Justice, which has been calling for worker

summary.phenomenon: Extreme heat, with temperatures expected to exceed 90°F (approximately 32.2°C), has been forecast for several cities hosting World Cup games.
summary.outlook: Labor advocates and scholars warn that workers making the World Cup possible could face serious heat-related health risks, potentially leading to numerous injuries if left unprotected.
